item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted. While there's definitely some lingering niggles like "what is this listed as in the database" and "how do i suspend my disbelief of this document's premise when I stop reading it?", those can't really be avoided if one wishes to execute this premise. I absolutely love the shift over time as you read this, and it's awareness of the reader's intelligence. Right as I'm raising the point that the implications of what I've read make it impossible, the "twist" hits and addresses it perfectly.

Like others have said, I would definately love more supplemental material, but I believe the one "picked as a preview" was perfect. It demonstrates the overall tone of the piece beautifully, and the ending revealing the first trapped was an O5 cements the sort of "power" of the entity, that even though someone that important was trapped in it, the foundation presumably still knows nothing or next to nothing about it.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The images used in this article are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). The images are as follows:

  • Public Domain Pictures
  • Pxhere
  • Public Domain pictures

item.title: Re: Incomplete object posted
item.content:encoded:
What is it missing?

Probably some greater context material, but the comment was posted almost half a year ago.

What is a hub?

Hub pages list collections or series of connected works meant to be read together. The relevant hub page for this piece is here: http://www.scp-wiki.net/aleph-null-hub

item.title: Re: Incomplete object posted
item.content:encoded: What is it missing? What is a hub?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that this is one of the few times that no/non-standard object class scps actually work well. Seriously I thought that the whole "THERE IS NO OBJECT CLASS" thing had no reason to exist. This article proved me wrong. Good job

item.title: Incomplete object posted
item.content:encoded: The hub seems to be incomplete, and yet this SCP has been posted in the main list. Why post a skip before it's even finished being written? -1, for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I lay on the grass, sometimes, if I listen very closely and everything else is quiet, I swear I can hear noises coming from far below the ground. They sound like some kind of clockwork mechanism, its gears quietly humming.

…Does this house, and the plane it occupies, belong to the Broken God?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's how they fire O5's =p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it that absurd that someone had a polaroid camera with them when they entered?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is good. The only REAL plothole is the image. It's literally impossible for this article to have an image - the first question that popped into my head was "i they're trapped inside how did they get that picture?" I would recommend you remove the image from the article, or replace it with one of the later images, because as it is it's an enormous and gaping plothole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello there. First off, gongrats on this very successful piece which I find myself very interesting.

Sorry for bringing it up again, but I'd like to second the point made above about the lathes.

As my father is a machinist and I've worked with him a lot, I know for a fact that all the craftmanship and skills related to the lathes, is something that these guys can be very proud of; the lathes itself is a very iconic unit within a machinery, at least it was during the pre-CNC era. It's like their flag or something.

Your reasoning about how the O5 person could ignore it, it makes sense and I fully understand the fact that the page as is, it works, so why change it?

Would the ignorance of staff to such matters though be something that you like to show in your writing? If you are not familiar with heavy machinery etc., look at what could be an O5 staff mistaking the capital city of a small state.

It's not critical but is it something you want to show in your SCP?

I think a big water tank with or without the pump showing would do nicely for the purposes of this picture and wouldn't really harm your SCP. Your hook is elsewhere anyway and it's too strong to get spoiled by that…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's funny, I read Introductory Antimemetics fairly recently and thought the idea there of "an SCP article that can only be read while under the area of effect by the SCP" was incredibly inspired. Come a few days later, and this skip with the same idea gets featured on the front page.

But whereas the concept in that Tale generated a sense of urgency, the same concept here is portrayed more mellow, with something… sad? beneath the surface. And I like that. Nothing complicated or life-threatening, but something that's unsettling by its nature but at the same time… calm, in a ways. Which I think compliments the structure here; without the proper equipment or expertise (and ultimately no need for it, considering the information won't leave the anomaly itself) to observe how exactly 2508, 2508-1, and 2508-2 operate in the typical clinical tone, it's written in an old school "here's a in-a-nutshell" sort of way.

I also think it's a nice touch how the notice at the top changes meaning once you get to the excerpt at the end.

Though, I am disappointed that there's no supplement of journal entries despite the miscellaneous section being mentioned. Doesn't mean there has to be a deeper or meaningful narrative added on top of an already interesting idea, but I feel like there's missed potential of the wide array of different reactions from people who have inhabited 2508.

In any case, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a bit of a necropost of 9 months but I like this idea of adding a footnote to the footnote of some snarky guy saying that there would be nobody to write how it is lethal because the observer will be dead and replaced. In a kind of crazed desperation of "what the fuck else am I going to do and think about" tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I had that exact thing pointed out to me a while ago, I had decided on not changing it for fear of messing about with it after it had been sorta "established" as an article (if that makes any sense).

Anyway, I'd like to offer a bit of hand-waving and say that it's entirely possible that SCP-2508-1 is in fact a modified lathe of sorts, and that the people who had stayed in SCP-2508 knew just about as much about lathes and pumps as I did when finding a public domain image to use :P

And if that's the case, they could have just called it a pump because they (like me) didn't know any better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know its not particulairly important, but i still feel like I should mention.

SCP-2508-1, As shown in the picture is a lathe. Not a pump like device.

+how a lathe works
-hide

A lathe is a machine used for precise fabricating of (round) metal parts by placing the metal piece in the round disk, shown slightly at the right in the picture. (wich is supposed to hold "claws" to secure the item.
The machine operates by spinning the disk, and the piece in it, powered by an electric motor and gearbox.

Once the piece is spinning the object shown in the center of the picture, situated on the rail, is used to edit the metal piece.

the object is desinged to hold a hardened metal "chisel" wich can be dialed into place with precicions of smaller than thousands of millimeters at modern lathes, allowing the lathe to shape a piece into the desired form by sellectively scraping the metal off the piece.

Lathes are also often equipped with all sorts of other helpfull features.
Like the object shown on the left side of the picture, inline with the disk or "clawplate", wich can hold a drill, so the user can drill a hole in the center of the piece.
Or a set of gears that can be used to automatically give the "chissel" a solid movement relative the RPM of the piece to create threading, like on a screw.

Lathes do often have pumps installed, but these are mostly for lubrication, and actually require the lathe to run before they will function.

The only pumps a lathe may have that involves water is a coolant pump to spray water over the piece to prevent it from getting too hot due to friction. But again, these require the lathe to run, and have no other reason to consume water over time.

Summed up: lathes aren't pumps…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Multiple grammatical errors throughout. Nonsensical syntax and redundant statements. Multiple instances of run on sentences.

The idea itself is not very well thought out either.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
second paragraph of the description:

as one exits the property of SCP-2508 on one end, they will find themselves arriving at the other end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens at the edge of the ".53 km2 of surrounding property"? Forcefield? Spatial loop? Black void? Probably you just discover yourself walking in through the front doorway of the house…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good reveal halfway in. Quite enjoyable from start to finish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. The entire SCP feels peaceful and… Kind of sad. I don't really get the subanomalies or their purpose, though. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this! It's creepy and perfectly captures the feelings of isolation. I think I speak for most people here when I say that this could be even better if you expanded, because I found myself wanting more when it ended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A very extended house-sitting. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Oswald is a prick. "The previous writer didn't explain how someone might die of doing this thing [which can only be done in a place that only has one occupant at a time]." No shit, Sherlock, maybe that's because if someone died doing it, there would be no witnesses to the death, just like there are no witnesses to anything else that happens to you here.

I like being able to characterize a contributor to the in-universe text that way. It's just one of many things that drive home the implications of life in this place. A normal SCP article has to maintain certain standards, because if the original researcher didn't do it right, someone would hold them accountable. This article is free from those restrictions, because there are no authorities in SCP-2508.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The gender of old and new need not be the same.

Of course! I mainly end up assuming they all have been women. This is arbitrary, mind you — it's just what happens after the character was a woman in stuff like… Ecce Perago as the woman with chopsticks in her hair, the scary woman in the mint-green pantsuit The Flytrap, Resurrection (same character as Flytrap, combined with Clef's benefactor from Termination Order), and Miriam Prayther in the absolutely terrifying SCP-2718.

Don't take this as critique, of course — I just thought it would be fun to point out.

I am excited to see what you come up with for the supplement, Eekium! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, let's remember that from the point of view of the Foundation, O5-7 disappeared and needed replacement with a new O5-7. The gender of old and new need not be the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm currently working on adding an archive as a supplemental page. So that should be along soon. As for the O5, unless I'm mistaken (which I might be) I don't think I strictly refer to O5-7 as either male or female.

Either way, thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A nice SCP, with the added appeal of being perfectly written in-universe (I could almost feel the cracks on the reader's mind as they got the implication that they were never gonna leave, and that even O5 have been trapped here, without any escape or rescue attempt ever being successful… but the note at the end, as previous comments mentioned, is somewhat jarring.

This needs to stay in the site. In what way to improve over it, and/or add to it, I'm not sure. Supplemental extracts from the "study archive", maybe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+100 in just a week, wow. I thought there was some strange glitch going on when I saw that at first! (Then I read it and understood why.)

I really like this for the same reasons as everyone. The tone of this, the way it sucks you in and mesmerizes you, is just amazing.

I also will echo suggestions others made. Certainly a +100 article doesn't require changes, but I know from experience that you can make a great article even greater.

I would agree with sirpudding's specific suggestion, and I would agree that it's a good idea to try writing more of the archive. It really depends on how deeply you feel comfortable going into the secrets and structure behind this place. But if you do feel comfortable with it, I'd absolutely recommend it.

…so tl;dr I agree with the people who recommended carrying on in a supplement. The article is a good length as-is; it could be slightly shortened or lengthened with no problem. But anything beyond that, and… why mess with what works?

(random side note, I very strongly assumed that O5-7 was a woman since all the most significant versions of that O5 that I'm aware of have been women. I prefer it that way, though it didn't actually affect much.)

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
I was going to +1 so hard until i got to the footnote,
Cleaning supplies? Really? Are you trying to be funny? It's just SO disappointing after i was going to +1 it went to a good article to a perfect one, and now i am incredibly disappointed that i can't +2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[COMMENTDELETED]
3/9/16

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Oh my god. This just made my evening. Thank you everybody!
Jesus this reached +100 fast. Congratulations Eekium!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a pretty solid idea, I'd like that presentation better. Were that I the author I'd make the change, but alas…!

The more I think about it, the more I like it. It reinforces the idea that a fair number of people have been through here and someone else found the note useful, and less like the O-5 deciding that their own blog post was important enough to separate from the archives and into the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't feel like digging through this thing's discussion and ruining it for myself, but this is fantastic. It reminds me of SCP-1411 in a good way. I really like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Chat is a tricksie beastie. :B But yeah, it's not often you type .lc and see +50 on something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think my issue isn't with the note, but with the "Oh hey guy, here's a random sample" way that it is introduced. If instead it was something like "I found the following entry to be a good place to start" or something that makes a point of it being this note specifically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks a bunch! I'll fix those little things. And this was generating buzz??? And I missed it?!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I started getting angry at "the reader may be growing aware", but I kept on going and suddenly it became magical. All similarities to other articles flew right out the window; I can see why this was generating so much buzz in the chat last night. That said, I found a few spots where wording could be tightened:

+ Show edits
– hide block

Both SCP-2508's builders and original inhabitants (if it is presumed there were any)

I think this should be "if it can be presumed".

so I wasn't terribly restrictive

Not sure what this is supposed to mean.

if listen very closely

Missing "I".

Lately the clouds keep rolling in, and the plants in the basement are buzzing more than usual.

And I'm just highlighting this because it's really, really good sci-fi, up there with the bright, cold day where the clocks were striking thirteen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm suprised that you didn't point out the fact and just insert it in the halfway. I like being played when reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you refer to a record archive and a supplemental section recording the thoughts of multiple occupants. That would have been a good opportunity to tell an extended story

I'd have liked to have seen that too. I was looking forward to more than there ended up being there, to be honest. It sounds like it could really interesting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much! I can see a tale of this totally happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
more supplemental material

Or a Tale?

This whole SCP feels like the intro to a larger story. It's an effective intro; I want to read the rest now. By itself though, I don't feel satisfied yet. Anyway, good luck!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The excerpt comes off as a decent summary and a good launching pad for diving into said archives. Also, it's from an O5, that carries a little bit more weight to it.

The article directs the reader to the archive, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another vote for a separate supplement page. It'll add to the story without risking making the article feel bloated. I like it as it is, but I'd certainly not be averse to seeing more of the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's probably what I'll end up doing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might suggest a supplemental page, rather than adding to this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think more supplemental material is an opportunity to improve the piece, depending on how you were to move forward on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly think some stuff more to help flesh it out would make it more engaging than it is, and I already love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So you think more supplemental material would help? I considered doing that while drafting it but decided against it because I didn't want the article to get too lengthy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Conceptually I like this, and it succeeds in making me vaguely uneasy with it. I feel like there's something lacking, and it might be more details about what living there is like, not sure.

The O5 note is introduced rather incongruously, why would the previous editor choose to excerpt a single entry rather than just direct the reader to the archive? The reader has their entire life to read it.

I like the hints that the dwellers are just housesitting for something with a very different sense of time.

I need to think about how I want to vote here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got the sense that it exists in the database in slot 2508, just as we're seeing it. Although the inhabitants assume that the outside world can't see it, we can.

If that's the case, that would only leave the question of whether the document is editable by the Foundation at large; I'd say that it isn't, otherwise we'd see two-way communication (not to mention an actual object class).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The hook was enough for me to settle in for what I expected to be a fairly lengthy supplemental section, but the payoff didn't really match the setup.

I really like the general idea of a document that's only meant for the in-universe researcher stuck in the anomaly. That opens the way for a non-traditional execution of the SCP format without necessarily going down the gimmicky format screw path. And the mood of the house is a nicely uncanny, sort of unsettling vibe, without being over-the-top pointlessly creepy.

The note from the O5 was kind of underwhelming. The dude sounds a little stuffy for someone who's not above getting shitfaced and taking a drive through town, doubly so for someone who's writing down his thoughts on a weird transdimensional trap house because he's got nothing else to do. More than that, though, you refer to a record archive and a supplemental section recording the thoughts of multiple occupants. That would have been a good opportunity to tell an extended story; not necessarily one that explicitly details what's going on here, but this could have gone much further down the rabbit hole than it did. I frequently criticize the "more is more" attitude that we all have lately, but this really could have been fleshed out.

I had initially no-voted this one because I liked how it began, but this just feels too incomplete for me to support. The article falls considerably short of the quality that it teases.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would work, except that format screw was pretty much exactly used in SCP-2204.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How would this display in their database? Would 2508 just be blank?

Yeah, I like this, but that detail kind of niggles. Not enough to downvote, mind. I'm not sure how you'd truly solve the quandary, but maybe start out with an article describing a mystery cottage that just swallows people and vanishes them, with an Out-of-Universe link to the actual doc? I don't know.

Anyway, as I said, I enjoyed this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good. Raises a lot of questions, but still manages to feel satisfying while maintaining mystery. The format screw was also well executed, I felt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God damn. I don't have anything more to say because I don't even know where to start, other than god damn. For my money, one of the best new articles in months.

+1

EDIT: I think I figured it out. I like that it's not some crazy eldritch horror or existential dread, or that there's even anything inherently dangerous here.

It's just melancholic silence. All you can really do is live out your time here, and die a quiet, lonely death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most likely 2508 exists in the main database in some shape or form. There's just probably not a lot there, as it's incredibly difficult to get information on something that can only be studied once you're eternally trapped inside it. Perhaps 2508 in the main database concerns a phenomenon of missing Foundation personnel, but even then, there'd be very little they'd have to go on.

So, to answer your questions: maybe. I tried to leave some room for doubt given that the article is written from the perspective of the people inside the anomaly, not out. So maybe The Foundation has no idea about this thing. Or maybe it's just a well kept secret.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Better than it seemed at first, but it raises some questions for me. Is it really possible the Foundation hasn't discovered this before? We have some skips with similar properties that the Foundation at large is aware of, instead of individual users.

How would this display in their database? Would 2508 just be blank?
